🧬 Encapsulin-Inspired Protein Cage Designs

RFdiffusion2 backbone generation × PRISM tetrahedral expansion

Biological Inspiration: Magnetotactic Bacteria Encapsulins
Magnetotactic bacteria (e.g., Magnetospirillum magneticum) produce encapsulin nanocompartments — icosahedral protein cages (T=1, 60 protomers, ~24 nm diameter) that store iron via ferroxidase cargo proteins. Each ~260-residue subunit adopts the conserved HK97-fold and self-assembles at a defined radial position to form the closed shell.
Our approach: We mimic this by generating 200-residue subunit backbones with RFdiffusion2 (SE(3) flow matching), positioning each at a 50 Å cage radius, then expanding with tetrahedral symmetry (12 subunits) using PRISM.

Design 0 — T-symmetric Cage (200 res/subunit)

Cage ⌀: 6.7 nm
Rg: 52.6 Å
Shell radius: 33.4–69.6 Å
Mean R: 52.0 Å
Min contact: 6.5 Å
Clashes (<2Å): 0
Residues: 2400 (12 chains)
Close interfaces: 6

Design 1 — T-symmetric Cage (200 res/subunit)

Cage ⌀: 4.0 nm
Rg: 54.0 Å
Shell radius: 20.1–79.7 Å
Mean R: 51.4 Å
Min contact: 3.9 Å
Clashes (<2Å): 0
Residues: 2400 (12 chains)
Close interfaces: 30

Design 2 — T-symmetric Cage (200 res/subunit)

Cage ⌀: 6.4 nm
Rg: 52.6 Å
Shell radius: 32.0–67.7 Å
Mean R: 51.7 Å
Min contact: 9.4 Å
Clashes (<2Å): 0
Residues: 2400 (12 chains)
Close interfaces: 6

Design 3 — T-symmetric Cage (200 res/subunit)

Cage ⌀: 4.4 nm
Rg: 52.9 Å
Shell radius: 22.1–71.2 Å
Mean R: 51.6 Å
Min contact: 10.1 Å
Clashes (<2Å): 0
Residues: 2400 (12 chains)
Close interfaces: 0

Pipeline: RFdiffusion2 backbone → radial positioning (50 Å) → PRISM T-symmetry expansion → cavity analysis
Designs are backbone-only (poly-ALA); sequence design via ProteinMPNN is the next step.
